ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Select medical condition code from Alphabetical Index -> icd (New Version): Select medical condition code from Tabular List
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Select substance code from corresponding subcategory -> icd (New Version): Select substance code from appropriate subcategory in Tabular List

findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) contain identical text regarding the hierarchy for assigning codes when use, abuse, and dependence are documented. There are no functional changes to the coding logic, priority rules, or specific behaviors described in the excerpts. The only distinction is the metadata label (Taxonomy description) which slightly varies in phrasing but conveys the same meaning, indicating an administrative update rather than a clinical guideline change.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Taxonomy description states 'prioritizes dependence over abuse, and both over simple use' -> icd (New Version): Taxonomy description states 'prioritizes assigning the code for dependence over abuse or use when multiple conditions are documented'
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Requirements state 'Provider documentation must explicitly refer to use, abuse, and/or dependence' -> icd (New Version): Requirements state 'Provider documentation must explicitly refer to use, abuse, or dependence'

findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) contain verbatim identical text regarding the requirement to link unspecified psychoactive substance codes to specific disorders like sexual dysfunction or sleep disorders. The new version adds administrative metadata such as 'Deprecated' and 'New items' sections to explicitly flag the prohibition of isolated use, but the core clinical logic and behavioral rules remain unchanged. This indicates a maintenance update to clarify existing rules rather than a substantive change in coding behavior.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes are restricted to cases where the unspecified substance use is linked to specific disorders like sexual dysfunction, sleep disorder, or mental/behavioral disorders. -> icd (New Version): Assignment requires verification that the unspecified code is not used in isolation without a documented associated condition.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): No explicit deprecation warnings or new item lists. -> icd (New Version): Includes explicit 'Deprecated' status for assigning codes without documented conditions and 'New items' mandating documentation of the specific relationship.

findings.section: Adverse Effect and Poisoning Code Reporting
findings.summary: The older version and the new version contain identical text regarding the definition of secondary diabetes causes and the requirement to follow sections I.C.19.e and I.C.20. The only difference is a minor addition in the new version's requirements section clarifying that coding must reflect whether the condition is a sequela or a direct adverse effect, which is already implied by the existing text. No clinical logic or code assignment rules have changed.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Secondary diabetes may be caused by an adverse effect of correctly administered medications, poisoning or sequela of poisoning. -> icd (New Version): Secondary diabetes may be caused by an adverse effect of correctly administered medications, poisoning or sequela of poisoning.

findings.summary: The two versions present the exact same clinical logic, sequencing requirements, and 7th character matching rules for traumatic coma scale coding. The text excerpts are identical, and the extracted profiles contain no substantive differences in behavior or constraints. The only distinction is the formatting of the 'Deprecated' and 'New items' lists, which reflects an administrative update in how the guidelines are presented rather than a change in the coding rules themselves.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Lists 'Reporting coma scores occurring after admission but before 24 hours as valid ICD-10-CM classifications' as deprecated. -> icd (New Version): Lists 'Reporting coma scores that occur after admission but within the first 24 hours as valid ICD-10-CM data' as deprecated.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Lists 'Assigning multiple coma scale codes for scores captured within the first 24 hours of admission' as deprecated. -> icd (New Version): Lists 'Assigning unspecified coma scale codes when specific subcategory codes (R40.21- to R40.24-) are available' as deprecated.

findings.summary: The core clinical logic regarding Y93 assignment, exclusions for poisonings/sequela, and the prohibition of Y93.9 remains unchanged between the two versions. The primary difference is the addition of a 'Deprecated' section in the new version to explicitly flag previously accepted but now discouraged practices (like recording multiple codes), which serves as an administrative clarification rather than a change in clinical rules.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Implicitly forbids multiple Y93 codes and Y93.9 usage -> icd (New Version): Explicitly lists 'Recording more than one Y93 code on a medical record' and 'Assigning Y93.9 when the activity is not stated' as deprecated practices.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): States activity codes are not applicable to poisonings, adverse effects, misadventures, or sequela -> icd (New Version): Repeats the exact same exclusion criteria without modification.

findings.section: Y92 Place of Occurrence Coding Guidelines
findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) present the exact same rules regarding Y92 secondary codes, including the single assignment at the initial encounter and the exception for new injuries during hospitalization. There are no clinical logic changes, code assignment restrictions, or definition expansions between the two texts; they are functionally identical. The only distinction is the metadata label (FY25 Guidelines vs. icd), indicating a standard administrative update or versioning without substantive content modification.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes from category Y92 are secondary codes used after other external cause codes to identify the location of the patient at the time of injury or other condition. -> icd (New Version): Codes from category Y92 are secondary codes used after other external cause codes to identify the location of the patient at the time of injury or other condition.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions present identical text and logic for assigning 7th character codes based on active treatment status versus routine healing care. There are no substantive changes to the clinical definitions, coding restrictions, or guideline requirements between the two excerpts. The only distinction is the source label (FY25 Guidelines vs. icd), indicating a static rule set without modification.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): 7th character A is for use as long as the patient is receiving active treatment for the fracture. -> icd (New Version): 7th character A is for use as long as the patient is receiving active treatment for the fracture.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): 7th character D is to be used for encounters after the patient has completed active treatment for the fracture. -> icd (New Version): 7th character D is to be used for encounters after the patient has completed active treatment for the fracture.

findings.summary: The older version (Chunk 43) and the new version (Chunk 475) contain identical text regarding the cause-and-effect relationship, the requirement for clinical significance, and the rule that the term 'complication' need not be explicitly documented. The only difference is a minor phrasing variation in the requirements section where 'not expected' was changed to 'not an expected outcome', which does not alter the clinical logic. Since the content is effectively identical, this represents an administrative update or versioning artifact rather than a substantive guideline change.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): The condition must not be expected or an integral part of the disease process. -> icd (New Version): The condition must not be an expected outcome or an integral part of the disease process.

findings.summary: The older version and the new version contain verbatim identical text regarding the prohibition of assigning Chapter 19 injury codes for injuries occurring during or resulting from a medical intervention. Both versions mandate the use of appropriate complication codes instead without any change in clinical logic, scope, or behavioral requirements. The only distinction is the metadata label (Taxonomy description) which reflects the same constraint in both instances.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Injury codes from Chapter 19 should not be assigned for injuries that occur during, or as a result of, a medical intervention. -> icd (New Version): Injury codes from Chapter 19 should not be assigned for injuries that occur during, or as a result of, a medical intervention.

findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) contain verbatim identical text regarding the usage of Code R99 for patients pronounced dead upon arrival. There are no functional changes to the clinical logic, coding rules, or behavioral expectations between the two versions. The only distinction is the source label, indicating a static guideline maintained across updates.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Code R99 is used only when a deceased patient is brought to a facility and pronounced dead upon arrival. -> icd (New Version): Code R99 is used only when a deceased patient is brought to a facility and pronounced dead upon arrival.

findings.summary: The older version (Chunk 316) and the new version (Chunk 744) contain exactly the same text regarding the sequencing of intraoperative complication codes. There is no change in clinical logic, code assignment rules, or behavioral requirements between these two specific excerpts. The similarity suggests these are identical guideline statements, possibly representing a static rule or a reiteration within a larger document update.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Intraoperative and postprocedural complication codes should be sequenced first, followed by a code for the specific complication if applicable. -> icd (New Version): Intraoperative and postprocedural complication codes should be sequenced first, followed by a code for the specific complication if applicable.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain identical text regarding the assignment of separate injury codes, restrictions on T07 codes in inpatient settings, and prohibitions on using traumatic injury codes for surgical wounds. There are no functional changes to the clinical logic, coding rules, or sequencing requirements between the two versions. The only distinction is the metadata label (FY25 Guidelines vs. icd), indicating a versioning or administrative update rather than a substantive guideline change.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign separate codes for each injury unless a combination code is provided. -> icd (New Version): Assign separate codes for each injury unless a combination code is provided.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Do not assign codes from category T07 (Unspecified multiple injuries) in the inpatient setting unless more specific information is unavailable. -> icd (New Version): Do not assign codes from category T07 (Unspecified multiple injuries) in the inpatient setting unless more specific information is unavailable.

findings.section: NIHSS and Acute Stroke Code Sequencing
findings.summary: The older and newer versions present identical clinical logic, requiring NIHSS codes (R29.7-) to be sequenced after acute stroke codes (I60-I63) and mandating the reporting of the initial documented score. Both versions explicitly reference Section I.B.14 for documentation by non-provider clinicians and allow facilities to capture multiple scores if desired. The only distinction is a minor phrasing variation in the 'New items' section of the newer profile, which reiterates the existing permission rather than introducing a new rule.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Stroke scale codes must be sequenced after the acute stroke diagnosis code(s). -> icd (New Version): Sequencing must place the acute stroke code before the NIHSS code.

findings.summary: The older version and the new version present the exact same core logic: code assignment is based on the provider's documentation of the relationship between the infection and the procedure. There are no substantive changes to the clinical rules, coding restrictions, or definitions between the two texts; the differences are limited to minor phrasing variations in the 'Requirements' and 'Deprecated' lists (e.g., 'result of' vs 'related to'). Since the clinical behavior and logic remain unchanged, this represents an administrative update or a restatement of existing guidelines rather than a functional change.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Provider documentation must explicitly state the infection is a result of the procedure. -> icd (New Version): Provider documentation must explicitly state the infection is related to the procedure.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Deprecated: Assigning postprocedural codes based solely on temporal proximity without documented causality. -> icd (New Version): Deprecated: Assigning postprocedural codes without explicit provider documentation of the relationship.

findings.summary: The older version (Chunk 178) and the new version (Chunk 606) contain identical text excerpts and behavioral requirements for assigning codes based on infarction, hemorrhage, and timing. The only variation is a minor wording adjustment in the 'Requirements' section of the new version ('specify the nature' vs 'distinguish'), which does not alter the clinical logic or code assignment rules. Since the core instructions and raw text are effectively the same, this represents an administrative update or data synchronization rather than a substantive guideline change.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Documentation must establish a clear causal link between the intervention and the stroke. -> icd (New Version): The clinical record must specify the nature of the cerebrovascular event (infarction vs. hemorrhage).
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Code assignment depends on whether the event was an infarction or hemorrhage. -> icd (New Version): Code assignment depends on whether the event was an infarction or hemorrhage.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions present the exact same clinical logic, code assignment rules, and documentation requirements for CMD without any logical delta. The raw text excerpts are identical, indicating these are likely the same guideline content indexed under different internal IDs or versions. Since the feature definition and behavior are unchanged, this represents an administrative update or data duplication rather than a clinical guideline modification.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Code I21.B is assigned for myocardial infarction with coronary microvascular disease | Code I21.B is assigned for myocardial infarction with coronary microvascular dysfunction | Code I21.B is assigned for myocardial infarction with non-obstructive coronary arteries (MINOCA) with microvascular disease -> icd (New Version): Code I21.B is assigned for myocardial infarction with coronary microvascular disease | Code I21.B is assigned for myocardial infarction with coronary microvascular dysfunction | Code I21.B is assigned for myocardial infarction with non-obstructive coronary arteries (MINOCA) with microvascular disease

findings.summary: The older version and the new version contain identical text regarding the assignment of I21.9 for unspecified cases and I21.3 for type 1 STEMI without site information. The only difference is a minor phrasing change in the requirements section where 'Site of infarction must be documented' was updated to 'Documentation must include the site of the infarction', which does not alter the clinical logic or code assignment rules. This indicates a formatting or administrative update rather than a substantive guideline change.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Site of infarction must be documented to assign a more specific code than I21.3. -> icd (New Version): Documentation must include the site of the infarction to avoid assigning I21.3 for STEMI.

findings.section: AMI Coding and tPA Status Rules
findings.summary: The older version and the new version contain identical text regarding the coding of nontransmural/subendocardial AMIs and tPA status rules. There are no behavioral changes, guideline reversals, or definition expansions between the two provided excerpts. The only distinction is the metadata label (FY25 Guidelines vs. icd), indicating a static rule set without clinical logic modification.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): If an AMI is documented as nontransmural or subendocardial, but the site is provided, it is still coded as a subendocardial AMI. -> icd (New Version): If an AMI is documented as nontransmural or subendocardial, but the site is provided, it is still coded as a subendocardial AMI.

findings.summary: The older version (Chunk 104) and the new version (Chunk 533) present the exact same text and logic regarding the designation of complications resulting from surgical procedures. There are no functional differences in the coding rules, requirements, or behavioral instructions between the two excerpts. The similarity suggests this is likely a consistency check or an administrative update where the guideline text was reaffirmed without substantive change.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Designate the complication as the principal diagnosis if treatment is directed at resolving it. -> icd (New Version): Designate the complication as the principal diagnosis if treatment is directed at resolving it.

findings.summary: The older version and the new version present the exact same definitions for Present on Admission (POA) status codes without any logical changes. Both versions mandate that 'Y' indicates presence at admission, 'N' indicates absence, 'U' is used for insufficient documentation, and 'W' is used when the provider cannot clinically determine the status. Since the clinical rules and behaviors are identical, this represents a stable guideline rather than a functional change.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Y = present at the time of inpatient admission -> icd (New Version): Y = present at the time of inpatient admission
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): U = documentation is insufficient to determine if condition is present on admission -> icd (New Version): U = documentation is insufficient to determine if condition is present on admission

findings.summary: The two versions present the exact same clinical logic and code assignment rules for Point of Admission reporting, including the specific definitions for 'Y', 'N', 'U', 'W', and the 'Unreported/Not used' exemption. While the newer version includes slightly more verbose phrasing regarding documentation justification and deprecated practices, the core behavioral output and exemption criteria remain unchanged. The differences are purely administrative refinements to the explanatory text rather than substantive changes to the coding logic itself.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign 'Y' (Yes) when the condition is present on admission | Assign 'N' (No) when the condition is not present on admission | Assign 'U' (Unknown) when the presence on admission cannot be determined | Assign 'W' (Clinically undetermined) when the condition is clinically undetermined | Mark as 'Unreported/Not used' to exempt the condition from POA reporting requirements -> icd (New Version): Assign 'Y' (Yes) when the condition is present on admission | Assign 'N' (No) when the condition is not present on admission | Assign 'U' (Unknown) when the presence on admission cannot be determined | Assign 'W' (Clinically undetermined) when the condition is clinically undetermined | Mark as 'Unreported/Not used' to exempt the condition from POA reporting requirements

findings.summary: The two versions contain identical core instructions regarding the assignment of POA indicators after code assignment and the requirement for provider accountability. Both explicitly reference the CDC website for the list of exempt codes and mandate querying providers for unclear documentation. The only variation is a minor phrasing difference in the 'Requirements' section where the older version lists 'detailed documentation' as a requirement while the newer version frames 'provider clinical judgment' as the primary determinant, but this does not alter the operational logic or assignment rules.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): The POA indicator is assigned to diagnosis and external cause codes after ICD-10-CM codes are assigned in accordance with official coding guidelines. -> icd (New Version): The POA indicator is assigned to diagnosis and external cause codes after ICD-10-CM codes are assigned in accordance with official coding guidelines.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): The guidelines supplement but do not replace the main body of ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting. -> icd (New Version): The guidelines supplement the main body of ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines but do not replace them or provide guidance on when a condition should be coded.

findings.section: Outpatient to Inpatient Principal Diagnosis Selection
findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) present the exact same three-tier logic for selecting the principal diagnosis: complications first, then the surgery reason, then unrelated conditions. The new version appears to be a reformatting of the older text, splitting the second bullet point into two distinct lines for clarity without altering the clinical logic, requirements, or hierarchy of the guidelines.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): The guideline lists the rule for unrelated conditions as a single bullet point following the surgery reason rule. -> icd (New Version): The guideline separates the rule for unrelated conditions into its own distinct bullet point, improving visual separation of the three decision paths.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): The text flows as a continuous list of three items. -> icd (New Version): The text is formatted with an explicit line break between the surgery reason rule and the unrelated condition rule, though the content remains identical.

findings.summary: The text content, behavioral rules, and requirements are verbatim identical between the older FY25 Guidelines and the new icd version. No clinical logic, coding restrictions, or definitions have changed; the update appears to be a maintenance or formatting consistency check rather than a substantive guideline revision.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): When a patient is admitted to an observation unit for a medical condition, which either worsens or does not improve, and is subsequently admitted as an inpatient of the same hospital for this same medical condition, the principal diagnosis would be the medical condition which led to the hospital admission. -> icd (New Version): When a patient is admitted to an observation unit for a medical condition, which either worsens or does not improve, and is subsequently admitted as an inpatient of the same hospital for this same medical condition, the principal diagnosis would be the medical condition which led to the hospital admission.

findings.section: POA and Chronic Condition Assignment Logic
findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) contain verbatim identical text regarding the assignment of 'Y' for acute conditions present at admission and 'Y' for chronic conditions diagnosed post-admission. There are no functional changes, clarifications, or restrictions between the two versions; the logic remains static. The only distinction is the metadata source (FY25 Guidelines vs. icd taxonomy), indicating an administrative update rather than a clinical logic change.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign 'Y' for acute conditions that are present at time of admission and N for acute conditions that are not present at time of admission. -> icd (New Version): Assign 'Y' for acute conditions that are present at time of admission and N for acute conditions that are not present at time of admission.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign 'Y' for chronic conditions, even though the condition may not be diagnosed until after admission. -> icd (New Version): Assign 'Y' for chronic conditions, even though the condition may not be diagnosed until after admission.

findings.section: Present on Admission Clinical Assignment Logic
findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) present the exact same clinical assignment logic for 'Y' and 'N' values regarding conditions present on admission. There are no functional differences in the rules, examples, or requirements between the two texts provided. The change is classified as an administrative update because the content remains static, likely reflecting a versioning or formatting update rather than a clinical guideline modification.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign 'N' if at least one clinical concept was not present on admission -> icd (New Version): Assign 'N' if at least one clinical concept was not present on admission
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign 'Y' if all clinical concepts were present on admission -> icd (New Version): Assign 'Y' if all clinical concepts were present on admission

findings.summary: The two versions present identical logic regarding the definition of 'Present on Admission' (timing of inpatient orders vs. outpatient encounters) and the assignment rules for principal, secondary, and external cause codes. The only discernible difference is a minor textual variation in the 'Requirements' section where the newer version adds the word 'complete' to the documentation criteria, which does not alter the clinical logic or coding rules. Since the core behavior, definitions, and constraints remain unchanged, this represents an administrative update or minor text refinement rather than a substantive guideline change.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Documentation must be consistent, present, and clear to avoid provider queries. -> icd (New Version): Documentation must be consistent, complete, and clear to avoid provider queries.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions present the exact same definition of principal diagnosis as the condition chiefly responsible for admission. Both explicitly state that ICD-10-CM conventions take precedence over UHDDS definitions and list the identical scope of non-outpatient settings. There are no changes in clinical logic, coding rules, or specific behaviors between the two texts.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): UHDDS definitions apply to all non-outpatient settings including acute care, short term, long term care, psychiatric hospitals, home health, rehab facilities, nursing homes, and hospice services. -> icd (New Version): UHDDS definitions apply to all non-outpatient settings including acute care, short term, long term care, psychiatric hospitals, home health, rehab facilities, nursing homes, and hospice services.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Coding conventions in the ICD-10-CM, the Tabular List and Alphabetic Index take precedence over these official coding guidelines. -> icd (New Version): ICD-10-CM coding conventions, the Tabular List, and Alphabetic Index take precedence over official coding guidelines when determining principal diagnosis.

findings.section: UHDDS Principal Diagnosis Definition for Observation-to-Inpatient Transfers
findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain identical definitions, behaviors, and requirements regarding the UHDDS principal diagnosis for this specific transfer scenario. There are no clinical logic changes, code assignment restrictions, or guideline reversals between the two texts provided. The only distinction is the metadata label (FY25 Guidelines vs. icd New Version), indicating a static rule carried forward without modification.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Applies UHDDS definition for observation-to-inpatient transfers following outpatient surgery. -> icd (New Version): Applies UHDDS definition for observation-to-inpatient transfers following outpatient surgery.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Requires condition established after study to be chiefly responsible for admission. -> icd (New Version): Requires condition established after study to be chiefly responsible for admission.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain identical behavioral instructions for assigning codes for specific infection types and severe sepsis (R65.2) with organ dysfunction. Both versions mandate the same documentation requirements and provider queries, indicating no change in clinical logic or code assignment rules. The differences found in the metadata (e.g., 'Deprecated' items in the older version) reflect administrative cleanup rather than a change in the active coding guidelines themselves.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Coding musculoskeletal conditions resulting from healed injuries to chapter 19 instead of chapter 13. -> icd (New Version): Coding musculoskeletal conditions resulting from healed injuries to chapter 13 instead of chapter 19.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Coding recurrent bone, joint, or muscle conditions to chapter 19 instead of chapter 13. -> icd (New Version): Coding recurrent bone, joint, or muscle conditions to chapter 13 instead of chapter 19.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions present the exact same core definition for Excludes2 notes, stating that the excluded condition is not part of the primary code but may coexist. While the newer version includes slightly more verbose phrasing in the 'New items' and 'Requirements' sections (e.g., 'Mandatory verification'), the actual clinical logic, deprecated behaviors, and raw text excerpts are functionally identical. There is no substantive change in the coding rule itself, only a reinforcement of the existing guidance.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicit permission to code both the primary and excluded conditions together. -> icd (New Version): Explicit permission to code both the primary condition and the excluded condition concurrently.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Requirement to assess clinical appropriateness for dual coding under Excludes2 notes. -> icd (New Version): Requirement to assess clinical appropriateness for dual coding when an Excludes2 note is present.

findings.summary: The core clinical logic remains unchanged between the older FY25 guidelines and the new version, both mandating the exclusion of superficial injury codes (e.g., abrasions) when a more severe injury is present at the same anatomical location. The newer version adds specific procedural requirements for coders, emphasizing the need to explicitly identify the presence of the more severe injury and review clinical notes to confirm the severity before applying the exclusion. This refinement reduces coder variance by clarifying the evidentiary standard required to trigger the exclusion rule, rather than altering the rule itself.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Superficial injuries such as abrasions or contusions are not coded when associated with more severe injuries of the same site. -> icd (New Version): Superficial injuries such as abrasions or contusions are not coded when associated with more severe injuries of the same site; however, the coder must explicitly identify the presence of the more severe injury and review clinical notes to confirm severity before applying the exclusion.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Coding focus shifts exclusively to the more severe injury present at the same anatomical location. -> icd (New Version): Only the code for the more severe injury should be assigned on the claim, contingent upon the clinical establishment of the associated injury's severity.

findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) contain verbatim identical text for the Chronic Disease Billing Frequency Rules feature. There is no change in clinical logic, code assignment restrictions, or reporting requirements between the two excerpts provided. The only distinction is the addition of a period at the end of the sentence in the new version, which constitutes a minor formatting adjustment rather than a substantive guideline change.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Chronic diseases treated on an ongoing basis may be coded and reported as many times as the patient receives treatment and care for the condition(s) -> icd (New Version): Chronic diseases treated on an ongoing basis may be coded and reported as many times as the patient receives treatment and care for the condition(s).

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain identical text regarding the use of M80 codes for osteoporosis-related fractures caused by minor trauma. There are no substantive changes to the clinical logic, code assignment rules, or behavioral requirements between the two versions. The only differences are minor formatting variations in the extracted profile summaries, such as the phrasing of 'New items' and 'Deprecated' sections, which do not alter the actual guideline content.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicit requirement to use M80 codes even in the presence of minor trauma if the patient has known osteoporosis. -> icd (New Version): Explicit requirement to use M80 codes regardless of the severity of the precipitating trauma if osteoporosis is known.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Deprecated using traumatic fracture codes for osteoporosis-related fractures when M80 is applicable. -> icd (New Version): Deprecated using traumatic fracture codes for osteoporosis-related fractures.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain identical text for the Rehabilitation Admission Coding Guidelines, including the same sequencing rules for hemiplegia and aftercare codes. There are no behavioral changes, logic updates, or new instructions between the two versions provided. The only distinction is the metadata label (Chunk 393 vs Chunk 826), indicating a data extraction or versioning artifact rather than a clinical guideline change.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Identical text regarding sequencing rehabilitation codes and aftercare usage. -> icd (New Version): Identical text regarding sequencing rehabilitation codes and aftercare usage.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Includes reference to Section I.C.21.c.7 and I.C.19.a. -> icd (New Version): Includes reference to Section I.C.21.c.7 and I.C.19.a.

findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) contain verbatim identical text regarding the lack of a mandatory timeframe for documenting POA conditions. There are no behavioral differences, code assignment changes, or logical reversals between the two excerpts provided. The only distinction is the metadata label (FY25 vs icd), indicating a static guideline maintained across versions without substantive clinical logic alteration.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): There is no required timeframe as to when a provider must identify or document a condition to be present on admission. -> icd (New Version): There is no required timeframe as to when a provider must identify or document a condition to be present on admission.

findings.section: Present on Admission Data Collection Mandate
findings.summary: The two versions present the exact same mandate requiring present on admission data collection for inpatient admissions to general acute care hospitals and other regulated facilities. There are no substantive changes to the clinical logic, code assignment rules, or the definition of the mandate between the older FY25 guidelines and the new version. The only variation is a minor phrasing adjustment in the requirements section regarding 'accurate coding' versus 'accurate documentation', which does not alter the operational rule.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Accurate coding requires clear documentation of the timing of condition onset relative to admission -> icd (New Version): Accurate documentation of condition status at the time of admission is required for valid coding

findings.summary: The older and newer versions present the exact same text regarding the inclusion of symptoms, signs, and abnormal results without a specific diagnosis. There are no behavioral changes, logic updates, or scope expansions between the two provided excerpts. The only distinction is the metadata label (FY25 Guidelines vs. icd), indicating a static definition rather than a clinical guideline evolution.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Chapter 18 includes symptoms, signs, abnormal results of clinical or other investigative procedures, and ill-defined conditions regarding which no diagnosis classifiable elsewhere is recorded. -> icd (New Version): Chapter 18 includes symptoms, signs, abnormal results of clinical or other investigative procedures, and ill-defined conditions regarding which no diagnosis classifiable elsewhere is recorded.

findings.summary: The older and newer versions contain verbatim identical text regarding the requirement to code the reason for surgery as the first-listed diagnosis even if the procedure is cancelled. There are no functional changes to the clinical logic, code assignment rules, or behavioral expectations between the two versions. The only distinction is a minor rephrasing of the 'Requirements' section in the new version, which does not alter the underlying guideline.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): The encounter must be classified as an outpatient surgery (same day surgery). -> icd (New Version): The patient must present for an outpatient (same day) surgery encounter.

findings.summary: The provided text for both the 'Older' and 'New Version' profiles is identical, containing the exact same raw text excerpt and behavioral requirements. There are no clinical logic changes, restrictions, or clarifications between the two versions as presented in the input data. The similarity is perfect, indicating either a static guideline or a duplicate entry in the dataset.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): For ambulatory surgery, code the diagnosis for which the surgery was performed. If the postoperative diagnosis is known to be different from the preoperative diagnosis at the time the diagnosis is confirmed, select the postoperative diagnosis for coding, since it is the most definitive. -> icd (New Version): For ambulatory surgery, code the diagnosis for which the surgery was performed. If the postoperative diagnosis is known to be different from the preoperative diagnosis at the time the diagnosis is confirmed, select the postoperative diagnosis for coding, since it is the most definitive.

findings.summary: The older version and the new version present identical logic for assigning dominant or nondominant status based on laterality (left, right, or ambidextrous). There are no functional differences in the coding rules, definitions, or requirements between the two texts provided. The only distinction is the source label (FY25 Guidelines vs. icd), indicating a static rule carried forward without modification.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): For ambidextrous patients, the default should be dominant. -> icd (New Version): For ambidextrous patients, the default should be dominant.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): If the left side is affected, the default is non-dominant. -> icd (New Version): If the left side is affected, the default is non-dominant.

findings.summary: The older version explicitly detailed the scenario where both eyes have the same type but different stages, instructing to report one code. The newer version removes this specific instruction and replaces it with a general rule to assign a code for each eye when stages differ, even if laterality is not distinguished. This change simplifies the guideline text by removing a redundant specific case that was already covered by the general rule in the new version, without altering the clinical logic or code assignment outcome.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Report only one code for the type of glaucoma with the appropriate seventh character for the stage when both eyes are documented as the same type and stage but no bilateral code exists. -> icd (New Version): Assign a code for the type of glaucoma for each eye with the seventh character for the specific stage when eyes have the same type but different stages and laterality is not distinguished.

findings.summary: The core clinical logic regarding the assignment of the highest degree code (e.g., T24.311-) remains unchanged between the two versions. The only differences are minor formatting adjustments in the extracted profile, such as the removal of specific deprecated examples and slight phrasing variations, which do not alter the operational rule.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicitly lists 'Coding multiple codes' and 'Assigning a code for the lowest degree' as deprecated behaviors -> icd (New Version): Condenses these into a single deprecated item 'Coding multiple codes' without listing the lowest degree exception separately.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Includes specific example 'e.g., for second and third degree burns of right thigh, assign only code T24.311-' in the raw text -> icd (New Version): Retains the exact same example in the raw text excerpt.

findings.section: Primary Injury Sequencing for Nerve and Vessel Damage
findings.summary: The core clinical logic regarding sequencing remains exactly the same in both versions, with the primary injury coded first if it causes minor nerve or vessel damage. The only variation is a minor phrasing adjustment in the 'Requirements' section of the newer version, changing 'distinct' to 'minor relative to the primary injury' to reinforce the existing rule without altering the coding behavior. Since the behavioral outcome and specific instructions are functionally identical, this represents a non-substantive administrative update rather than a guideline change.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Additional codes for nerve or vessel injuries must be assigned only if the damage is distinct from the primary injury. -> icd (New Version): Additional codes for nerve or vessel injuries must be assigned only when the damage is minor relative to the primary injury.

findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) present the exact same sequencing rules for 'use additional code' notes, 'code first' notes, and bacterial infection categories B95/B96. The text excerpts provided for both versions are identical, indicating no change in clinical logic or coding behavior. The only distinction is the metadata label (FY25 Guidelines vs. icd), suggesting this is an administrative update or a re-publication of the same guideline content.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): 'Use additional code' notes indicate that a secondary code should be added if known to fully describe a condition. -> icd (New Version): 'Use additional code' notes indicate that a secondary code should be added if known to fully describe a condition.

findings.summary: The older version and the new version present the exact same clinical logic, requiring dehydration to be sequenced first when only rehydration is treated. The text excerpts are identical, indicating that the change is purely administrative or a reiteration of existing guidelines rather than a modification of the rule itself. No new restrictions, clarifications, or reversals were introduced in the newer version regarding this specific feature.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): When the admission/encounter is for management of dehydration due to the malignancy and only the dehydration is being treated (intravenous rehydration), the dehydration is sequenced first, followed by the code(s) for the malignancy. -> icd (New Version): When the admission/encounter is for management of dehydration due to the malignancy and only the dehydration is being treated (intravenous rehydration), the dehydration is sequenced first, followed by the code(s) for the malignancy.

findings.summary: The older version and the new version contain the exact same text regarding the assignment of Y90 codes based on documented blood alcohol levels and F10 conditions. There are no behavioral differences, clarifications, or restrictions between the two provided excerpts; they are functionally identical. The only distinction is the source identifier (Chunk 144 vs Chunk 574), which suggests a data indexing or administrative update rather than a change in clinical coding logic.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): A code from category Y90 may be assigned when blood alcohol level is documented and the provider has documented a condition classifiable to category F10. -> icd (New Version): A code from category Y90 may be assigned when blood alcohol level is documented and the provider has documented a condition classifiable to category F10.

findings.summary: The two versions present the exact same definition for 'other diagnoses,' requiring conditions to impact patient care through evaluation, treatment, procedures, stay length, or nursing intensity. Both explicitly cite UHDDS item #11-b to include coexisting or subsequent conditions while excluding those unrelated to the current episode. The scope of applicability is identical in both texts, covering acute care, short-term, long-term, psychiatric, home health, rehab, nursing home, and hospice settings. There are no functional differences in the coding logic or reporting requirements between the two provided excerpts.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): UHDDS definitions apply to inpatients in acute care, short-term, long term care and psychiatric hospital setting. -> icd (New Version): UHDDS definitions apply to inpatients in acute care, short-term, long term care and psychiatric hospital setting.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Provider is responsible for listing diagnoses in the patient record when ICD-10-CM Index or Tabular List provide no direction. -> icd (New Version): Provider is responsible for listing diagnoses in the patient record when ICD-10-CM Index or Tabular List provide no direction.
